Grouped Distributions

Goal

This notebook shows how to compare continuous distributions across groups.

The goal is not only to compare averages. We also care about spread, shape, and overlap.

Analytic Questions

We will ask:

  • How does adult systolic blood pressure vary by age group?
  • What do box plots show well?
  • What do violin plots show differently?

Load Data

nhanes_path <- "data/real/nhanes_2021_2023_analysis.csv"

nhanes <- read_csv(nhanes_path, show_col_types = FALSE)

nhanes |>
  glimpse()
Rows: 11,933
Columns: 57

Prepare Data

adult_bp <- nhanes |>
  filter(
    age_years >= 18,
    !is.na(systolic_mean),
    !is.na(age_group)
  )

adult_bp |>
  count(age_group)
# A tibble: 5 × 2
  age_group     n
  <chr>     <int>
1 18-29       882
2 30-44      1298
3 45-64      2005
4 65-79      1618
5 80+         320

Box Plot

Start with a grouped mapping.

p1 <- ggplot(
  adult_bp,
  aes(x = age_group, y = systolic_mean)
)

p1

Add a box plot.

p2 <- p1 +
  geom_boxplot()

p2

Add labels and a theme.

p3 <- p2 +
  labs(
    title = "Adult Systolic Blood Pressure By Age Group",
    subtitle = "Box plots show median, middle 50%, and potential outliers",
    x = "Age group",
    y = "Mean systolic blood pressure"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

p3

Interpretation: older age groups tend to have higher median systolic blood pressure, but there is still substantial overlap across groups.

Add Points Carefully

Points can show the observations, but they need transparency when there are many rows.

p1 <- ggplot(
  adult_bp,
  aes(x = age_group, y = systolic_mean)
) +
  geom_boxplot(outlier.shape = NA)

p1
p2 <- p1 +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.15, alpha = 0.15, size = 0.8)

p2
p3 <- p2 +
  labs(
    title = "Adult Systolic Blood Pressure By Age Group",
    subtitle = "Jittered points show the individual readings behind the boxes",
    x = "Age group",
    y = "Mean systolic blood pressure"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

p3

Violin Plot

Violin plots emphasize distribution shape within each group.

p1 <- ggplot(
  adult_bp,
  aes(x = age_group, y = systolic_mean)
)

p1
p2 <- p1 +
  geom_violin(fill = "gray85")

p2
p3 <- p2 +
  geom_boxplot(width = 0.12, outlier.shape = NA)

p3
p4 <- p3 +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution Shape By Age Group",
    subtitle = "Violins show shape; box plots show median and middle spread",
    x = "Age group",
    y = "Mean systolic blood pressure"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

p4

Group Summaries

Summaries help check what the plot suggests.

age_group_summary <- adult_bp |>
  group_by(age_group) |>
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    mean_systolic = mean(systolic_mean),
    median_systolic = median(systolic_mean),
    iqr_systolic = IQR(systolic_mean),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

age_group_summary
# A tibble: 5 × 5
  age_group     n mean_systolic median_systolic iqr_systolic
  <chr>     <int>         <dbl>           <dbl>        <dbl>
1 18-29       882          112.            111          14.6
2 30-44      1298          115.            114.         16.7
3 45-64      2005          124.            123.         20.7
4 65-79      1618          129.            127          24.7
5 80+         320          135.            134          29.0

Takeaways

  • Grouped distribution plots compare more than group averages.
  • Box plots are compact and good for median/spread comparisons.
  • Violin plots show more shape, but can be harder for new readers.
  • Add points only when they improve the plot rather than clutter it.
